Q62.intro:
Introduction

Involution of the uterus is the process by which the gravid uterus returns to its pre-pregnant state following delivery. This remarkable physiological process is essential to postnatal recovery and is a key parameter monitored by the nurse midwife during the puerperium. Failure or delay in involution signals underlying pathology requiring prompt intervention.
Q62.conclusion:
Conclusion

The nurse midwife must assess uterine fundal height and consistency at every postnatal visit to confirm normal involution. Early recognition of subinvolution — characterised by a soft, high, tender uterus and persistent lochia — is critical to preventing haemorrhage and sepsis. Timely intervention aligned with MUHS postnatal care protocols ensures maternal safety and recovery.

Q63.intro:
Introduction

Lochia is the vaginal discharge that occurs following delivery, representing the shedding of the decidua, blood, and necrotic tissue from the uterus during the puerperium. Systematic assessment of lochia — its type, amount, colour, and odour — is a fundamental nursing duty that provides vital information about uterine healing. Abnormal lochia may indicate retained products, infection, or subinvolution.
Q63.conclusion:
Conclusion

The nurse midwife must educate the postnatal mother about the normal progression of lochia from rubra → serosa → alba and instruct her to report offensive odour, heavy bleeding, or passage of clots. Accurate lochia assessment is an INC-mandated postnatal skill that directly prevents maternal morbidity from PPH and puerperal sepsis. Documentation of lochia findings at every postnatal visit underpins safe, evidence-based midwifery care.

Q64.intro:
Introduction

Postnatal exercises are a structured set of physical activities commenced after childbirth to restore musculoskeletal tone, promote circulation, and accelerate recovery of the pelvic floor and abdominal muscles. They form an integral component of postnatal care recommended by MUHS and the Indian Nursing Council, beginning within hours of delivery when the mother's condition permits. Early mobilisation and targeted exercises significantly reduce the risk of thromboembolism, urinary incontinence, and prolonged physical debility.
Q64.conclusion:
Conclusion

The nurse midwife plays a pivotal role in motivating and guiding postnatal women through progressive exercises, emphasising Kegel exercises for pelvic floor rehabilitation and deep breathing for circulatory recovery. Patient education must account for the Indian cultural context, where prolonged bed rest is traditional, and the midwife must gently correct misconceptions. Timely initiation and consistent follow-up of postnatal exercises are hallmarks of quality midwifery care under MUHS standards.

Q68.intro:
Introduction

Breastfeeding is the optimal method of infant nutrition, universally endorsed by the WHO, UNICEF, and the National Health Mission of India as the cornerstone of child survival and maternal recovery. It provides the newborn with colostrum — a biologically unique secretion rich in immunoglobulins — followed by mature milk that meets all nutritional needs for the first six months of life. The nurse midwife is the primary facilitator of successful breastfeeding initiation and continuation in the postnatal period.
Q68.conclusion:
Conclusion

The nurse midwife must initiate breastfeeding within one hour of birth, ensure correct latch and positioning, and counsel mothers on demand feeding, exclusive breastfeeding for six months, and continued breastfeeding up to two years per WHO guidelines. Addressing barriers such as perceived milk insufficiency, sore nipples, and cultural taboos is essential in the Indian healthcare context. Promotion of breastfeeding is an INC & MUHS priority that directly reduces infant mortality and supports maternal uterine involution.

Q69.intro:
Introduction

Postnatal (postpartum) mood disorders encompass a spectrum from the transient postnatal blues to the more severe postpartum depression and the rare but life-threatening puerperal psychosis. Postpartum depression affects approximately 10–15% of mothers globally, with higher prevalence noted in low-resource settings in India due to socioeconomic stressors, gender bias, and lack of support systems. Early identification and sensitive management by the nurse midwife are essential to safeguard both maternal mental health and infant wellbeing.
Q69.conclusion:
Conclusion

The nurse midwife must routinely screen all postnatal women using the Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) and refer those scoring above the threshold for psychiatric evaluation and counselling. Creating a non-judgmental environment, providing psychosocial support, and involving the family are critical nursing interventions in the Indian context. MUHS emphasises that mental health assessment is an inseparable component of holistic postnatal care and must never be overlooked.

Q107.intro:
Introduction

Subinvolution of the uterus is a significant postnatal complication in which the normal process of uterine regression is delayed or incomplete, predisposing the mother to secondary postpartum haemorrhage and puerperal infection. It is commonly encountered in resource-limited settings in India where antenatal anaemia, malnutrition, and multiparity increase susceptibility. The nurse midwife is ideally positioned to detect subinvolution during routine postnatal assessments and initiate timely referral.
Q107.conclusion:
Conclusion

Vigilant postnatal monitoring of uterine fundal height, lochia, and uterine consistency by the nurse midwife remains the most effective strategy for early detection of subinvolution. Prompt medical management with uterotonics and treatment of underlying infection can prevent life-threatening haemorrhage. MUHS postnatal care protocols mandate that every mother be assessed for signs of subinvolution before discharge and at each follow-up visit.

Q66.def:
Definition

Puerperal Pyrexia: A temperature of 38°C (100.4°F) or more occurring on any two of the first ten days postpartum, exclusive of the first 24 hours, when measured orally at least four times daily (WHO / MUHS definition).
Q66.conclusion:
Conclusion

The nurse midwife must monitor temperature four-hourly in the postnatal period, identify the source of infection early, and initiate appropriate antibiotic therapy as prescribed while ensuring strict aseptic technique. Puerperal sepsis remains a leading cause of maternal mortality in India and demands urgent, protocol-driven management. MUHS emphasises that meticulous perineal hygiene education and timely recognition of pyrexia are non-negotiable nursing responsibilities in the puerperium.

Q67.def:
Definition

Mastitis: Inflammation of the breast parenchyma, most commonly caused by Staphylococcus aureus entering through a cracked nipple, presenting with localised pain, swelling, erythema, and fever, usually within the first 4–6 weeks postpartum. If untreated, it may progress to a breast abscess — a localised collection of pus requiring surgical drainage.
Q67.conclusion:
Conclusion

The nurse midwife must educate mothers on correct latch technique and nipple care to prevent cracked nipples — the primary portal of entry for mastitis. Early antibiotic therapy and continued breastfeeding (or expression) are cornerstones of management to prevent progression to abscess. MUHS & INC standards require the nurse midwife to recognise mastitis promptly and refer for surgical consultation when fluctuation indicating abscess formation is detected.

Q103.def:
Definition

Puerperal Psychosis: A severe, acute psychiatric disorder arising within the first two weeks following childbirth, characterised by rapidly evolving symptoms of confusion, hallucinations, delusions, mania, or severe depression, constituting a psychiatric emergency requiring immediate hospitalisation and treatment.
Q103.conclusion:
Conclusion

The nurse midwife must recognise the early warning signs of puerperal psychosis — including sleeplessness, bizarre behaviour, and confusion — and arrange immediate psychiatric referral, as delay poses a risk of infanticide and maternal self-harm. Constant supervision, a calm environment, and family psychoeducation are critical nursing interventions. MUHS highlights puerperal psychosis as a postnatal emergency where the nurse midwife's prompt action can be life-saving for both mother and newborn.

Q104.def:
Definition

Secondary Postpartum Haemorrhage (Secondary PPH): Abnormal or excessive bleeding from the birth canal occurring between 24 hours and 12 weeks after delivery, most commonly caused by retained products of conception or uterine infection (endometritis).
Q104.conclusion:
Conclusion

The nurse midwife must educate every postnatal mother to report any sudden increase in vaginal bleeding, passage of clots, or offensive discharge after discharge from hospital. Immediate assessment, IV access, oxytocics, and urgent referral form the emergency nursing response to secondary PPH. MUHS emphasises that secondary PPH is a preventable cause of maternal mortality and that vigilant postnatal follow-up by the nurse midwife is the key to early detection and survival.

Q105.def:
Definition

Retained Products of Conception (RPOC): The incomplete expulsion of placental tissue, membranes, or fetal parts following delivery or abortion, confirmed on ultrasonography as endometrial echogenic material, which predisposes to secondary PPH, uterine infection, and subinvolution.
Q105.conclusion:
Conclusion

The nurse midwife must meticulously examine the placenta and membranes immediately after delivery to confirm completeness, as this is the primary preventive step against retained products. Any suspicion of incomplete placenta should trigger urgent obstetric review and ultrasound confirmation. MUHS protocols mandate thorough third-stage assessment as a core midwifery competency, with prompt surgical evacuation arranged when RPOC are confirmed to prevent life-threatening haemorrhage and sepsis.

Q106.intro:
Introduction

Perineal care in the puerperium encompasses the hygienic, therapeutic, and rehabilitative measures applied to the perineum following childbirth, particularly when episiotomy, perineal tears, or instrumented delivery has occurred. The perineum is highly susceptible to infection and delayed healing due to its proximity to the anorectal area, moisture, and lochia, making diligent care a cornerstone of postnatal nursing practice. Effective perineal care reduces the risk of wound infection, dehiscence, and long-term morbidity such as dyspareunia and pelvic floor dysfunction.
Q106.def:
Definition

Perineal Care (Puerperium): A set of nursing interventions aimed at maintaining cleanliness, promoting healing, preventing infection, and providing comfort to the perineal region following vaginal delivery, including wound assessment, hygiene practices, pain relief measures, and patient education throughout the postnatal period.
Q106.conclusion:
Conclusion

The nurse midwife must assess the perineum at every postnatal visit using the REEDA scale (Redness, Oedema, Ecchymosis, Discharge, Approximation) and educate the mother on front-to-back cleansing, pad hygiene, and early reporting of signs of infection. Adequate pain management through analgesics, ice packs, and sitz baths promotes compliance with hygiene and mobility. MUHS & INC recognise perineal care as a fundamental intrapartum and postnatal nursing skill that directly influences maternal recovery and quality of life.
